coredata.dc:title: High-performing structural optimization of graphene quantum dots as glyphosate herbicide photoluminescent probes: real case studies and mechanism insights

coredata.dc:description: The widespread usage of the herbicide glyphosate calls for urgent action, aiming at the development of new, simple, low-cost, and eco-friendly detection approaches. In the last decade, investigation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as potential optical probes for various pollutants rapidly grew, thanks to their easy-manipulative structure, remarkable photoluminescence (PL) in the visible part of the spectrum, good dispersibility, biocompatibility, and non-toxicity, as well. Herein, a fast, simple, and environmentally friendly method for GQDs structural modification is presented. GQDs raw powder was exposed to γ- rays at three different doses (100, 200, and 300 kGy) in air, without any solvent or reagents. Irradiation of dots under such affordable conditions led to the additional incorporation of oxygen-containing moieties in the GQD structure. For the first time, oxygen-rich GQDs irradiated at a 300 kGy dose were successfully applied as direct turn-off PL probe for glyphosate detection. The high coefficient of determination (R-squared (R2) = 0.99) and very low limit of detection (3.02 μmol L-1) reveal good linearity between the potential sensor and analyte, as well as sensitivity. Glyphosate was successfully detected in celery samples, with a recovery value of 107 ± 0.85%. To evaluate the biological safety of the proposed sensing probe, [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] (MTT) and the hemolysis assays were performed. Obtained results show that irradiated and non-irradiated GQDs did not cause the death of MRC-5 cells, and hemolysis of erythrocytes. The obtained results demonstrate that GQDs irradiated in an air medium can be potentially applied for glyphosate detection.

The authors acknowledge the Electron and Light Microscopy Service Unit of the School of Mathematics and Science of the Carl von Ossietzky University for the use of the JOEL 2100\u2009F 200\u2009kV TEM facility. Experimental details and the total contents of functional groups in all GQDs samples obtained by back potentiometric titrations experiment, titration curves of GQDs\u03B3air samples, optimized geometries of the glyph/GQD complexes, and absorption spectrum of glyph/emission spectra of GQDs\u03B3air-300\u2009kGy are discussed in the text.

DLS diameter distribution curves of GQDsγair samples in water.

High-resolution spectra of O 1 s and C 1 s region for GQDs samples at different doses of gamma irradiation.

UV-Vis spectra of GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy.
PL spectra of GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy.
The values of PL intensity maximums of the GQDsγair-100 kGy (a), GQDsγair-200 kGy (b), and GQDsγair-300 kGy (c), at different pH values.
Maximums of PL intensities of the GQDsγair-100 kGy (a), GQDsγair-200 kGy (b), and GQDsγair-300 kGy (c) recorded after UV light exposure (λ = 365 nm) in the different time intervals (0–300 min).
Titration curves of p-GQDs (1 mg mL-1) and NaOH (a) and Na2CO3 (b) after titration with HCl solution. For each sample curves of corresponding control with base blank are presented.

PL spectra of GQDsγair-300 kGy in the presence of increasing glyph concentrations (0 – 100 μmol L-1 (a), and dependence A0/A of glyph concentration graph with linear fitting curve (inset) (b). Spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and pH 7.

(a) Maxima of GQDsγair-300 kGy PL intensity in the absence (blank), and the presence of the various analytes (glyph, CF, AMT, Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Zn2+) in the concentration of 100 μmol L-1. (b) PL intensity % of GQDsγair-300 kGy PL intensity in the presence of the glyph, glyph and glucose, and glyph and citric acid in the concentration of 100 μmol L-1. λexc was 360 nm. All measurements were conducted at pH 7.
Extract of celery sample from a QuEChERS tube. The inset shows a homogenized celery sample in a QuEChERS tube before centrifugation.
FTIR spectra of GQDsγair-300 kGy (a) and GQDsγair-300 kGy in the presence of glyph (b).
The optimized geometries of the five glyph/GQD complexes as calculated at ωB97xD/6–31+G(d,p)/SMD(water) level of theory.

a) MEP plots of isolated glyph and GQD fragments and E1 and S1 complexes b) the NBO charges of the same species represented by colored atoms in a fixed color range. The dipole moment vectors (Debye) are indicated.

Cytotoxicity of GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, GQDsγair-300 kGy, and p-GQD toward MRC-5 cells. Cells were treated for 24 h with indicated concentrations of each GQD. Values are normalized to vehicle control expressed as 100%. Data are represented as means ± SD of three independent experiments performed in triplicate. Asterisk (*) indicates p<0,05.

Quantitative and qualitative assessment of hemolysis assay of GQDsγair-100 kGy (a), GQDsγair-200 kGy (b), GQDsγair-300 kGy (c), and p-GQD (d). Hemolysis ratio of human RBCs upon incubation with indicated concentrations (1, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 µg mL-1) of each GQD sample. Data are presented as mean ± SEM*
* SEM - Standard error of the mean. of three independent experiments performed in duplicate. Asterisk (*) indicates p<0.05. C+ represents positive and C- negative control reactions.
Average heights and diameters of GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy obtained by AFM analysis.
The results of ζ-potential measurements GQDsγair samples.
Quantitative findings and relative abundance for observed functional groups. Oxygen content is obtained by subtracting the interfering Na KLL species.
Data obtained by PL spectroscopy analysis of the GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy samples.

The total contents of functional groups in the p-GQDs, GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, GQDsγair-300 kGy samples calculated for 6.000 mL of GQDs dispersion (1 mg mL-1) obtained by back potentiometric titrations experiment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread usage of the herbicide glyphosate calls for urgent action, aiming at the development of new, simple, low-cost, and eco-friendly detection approaches. In the last decade, investigation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as potential optical probes for various pollutants rapidly grew, thanks to their easy-manipulative structure, remarkable photoluminescence (PL) in the visible part of the spectrum, good dispersibility, biocompatibility, and non-toxicity, as well. Herein, a fast, simple, and environmentally friendly method for GQDs structural modification is presented. GQDs raw powder was exposed to γ- rays at three different doses (100, 200, and 300 kGy) in air, without any solvent or reagents. Irradiation of dots under such affordable conditions led to the additional incorporation of oxygen-containing moieties in the GQD structure. For the first time, oxygen-rich GQDs irradiated at a 300 kGy dose were successfully applied as direct turn-off PL probe for glyphosate detection. The high coefficient of determination (R-squared (R2) = 0.99) and very low limit of detection (3.02 μmol L-1) reveal good linearity between the potential sensor and analyte, as well as sensitivity. Glyphosate was successfully detected in celery samples, with a recovery value of 107 ± 0.85%. To evaluate the biological safety of the proposed sensing probe, [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] (MTT) and the hemolysis assays were performed. Obtained results show that irradiated and non-irradiated GQDs did not cause the death of MRC-5 cells, and hemolysis of erythrocytes. The obtained results demonstrate that GQDs irradiated in an air medium can be potentially applied for glyphosate detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the constant growth in the human population, the need for food resources also exponentially increases [1]. According to the Food and Agriculture Organizations of the United Nations (FAO), the global agricultural land area is approximately 38% of the global land surface [2]. Considering that the preservation during the growth phase, yield, and quality of seeds are very important, humans have been using different natural and synthetic pesticides for protection. One of the most influential pesticides in the modern era of agricultural management is glyphosate (glyph). Glyphosate (N-[phosphonomethyl]glycine) is an organophosphorus, non-selective, broad-spectrum herbicide, commonly used in weed control [3]. Glyph was discovered in 1950, but for the first time was introduced to the market in 1974 as an active substance in Monsanto’s Roundup® [4]. With the appearance of glyphosate-resistant, genetically modified crops in 1990, the application of glyph-based herbicides dramatically increased around the world. Several studies published during the last decade have pointed out that glyph has already stepped into the food chain, it is persistently occurring in environmental media as well as human blood, and urine [5]. In 2015, the International Agency for Research of Cancer (IARC) classified glyph as “probably cancerogenic to humans” [5].

Common analytical techniques for pesticide detection such as high-performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography combined with mass spectrometry, enzyme-linked immune sorbent assays require complex equipment, and time-consuming sample preparation [6]. Also, the QuEChERS (Quick, Easy, Cheap, Effective, Rugged, & Safe) method is widely used [7]. In this respect, new technologies and tools that enable simple and economic instrumentation, high sensitivity, selectivity, repeatability, and practical application are continuously investigated [8]. Up to now, many optical and electrochemical methods based on voltammetry [9], colorimetry [10], electrochemiluminescence [8], and photoluminescence [11] have been reported in the literature for glyph detection. Recently, the scientific community focused its attention on carbonaceous nanomaterials, due to their outstanding stability, optical and electronic properties, and biocompatibility [12]. Since their discovery by Ponomarenko and Geim in 2008 [12], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been the focus of many research fields such as energy conversion and storage [13], photodynamic and photothermal therapy [14], drug delivery [15], bioimaging [16] as well as sensing [17]. GQDs may be considered as 0D carbon nanomaterials, whose structure is based on a graphene-like core and oxygen-contained functional moieties (namely carboxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, and hydroxy) localized at the surface and the edges of the aromatic core. The graphene backbone of dots is built from sp2-hybridized carbon atoms organized in a honeycomb network [18]. Due to oxygen moieties, their dispersibility in water and polar solvents reaches values of more than 2 mg mL-1 [19]. GQDs exhibit extraordinary electrical and optical properties such as semiconducting behavior (band gap values vary from 1.5 to 3.5 eV) [18,20], high absorption in the UV region of the spectrum, tunable, and intense photoluminescence (PL) in the visible part of the electromagnetic radiation, as well as photostability. Furthermore, GQDs are considered biocompatible and non-toxic nanomaterial [16,21,22].

Because of all these features, GQDs were examined in optical photoluminescence detection of different types of analytes such as metal ions [17,23], biomolecules [24], polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [25], antibiotics [26], and certain pesticides [17,27–29]. Jimenez-Lopez and co-workers have detected a glyph using the GQDs-AgNPs system as a fluorescent probe [30]. Glyph decreased GQDs-AgNPs PL intensity in the concentration range of 30–2000 ng mL-1, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 9 ng mL-1. Furthermore, the glyph was detected in a turn-off complex mechanism using SO4 2--loaded Fe,N-co-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs), using acetylcholinesterase enzyme as an intermediate substance [31]. In this case, LOD was evaluated as 0.066 ng mL-1. Recently, Li et al. used N-CQDs in a turn-off/turn-on system as a dual PL probe for Cu2+ ions and glyph [31]. In the presence of increasing concentration of Cu2+, the PL intensity of dots linearly decreased, while with the addition of glyph in the system, the PL intensity was renewed. LOD for glyph was 0.049 μg mL-1. As far as we know, a PL sensor for the direct detection of glyphosate based on GQDs has not yet been developed.

Establishing interactions between a potential sensor and analyte is one of the key elements. To achieve this, the GQD structure was modified by the incorporation of different heteroatoms and functional groups [32–35]. The aim of functionalization is the creation of new analyte-binding sites on GQDs; this is usually achieved by a chemical approach, utilizing strong oxidants and/or hazardous chemicals [36]. Improper disposal of residual chemicals leads to environmental pollution. At the beginning of the 21st century, an investigation of the effects of γ rays on carbon nanomaterials was started [37–40]. Graphene oxide and GQDs are doped with nitrogen at different irradiation doses in the range of 5–200 kGy [41,42] and 25–200 kGy [23,27,43], respectively. Ethylenediamine or L-cysteine was used as a source of N atoms, but also γ irradiation of carbon materials can also be applied in various solvents such as isopropyl alcohol [18], ethanol/water mixture [44], and others [45].

For the first time, GQDs were modified by an ultra-fast, solvent-free, and simple approach by γ irradiation in the air. Pristine GQDs were produced by the electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrodes followed by gamma irradiation in the solvent-free medium at three different doses (100, 200, and 300 kGy). The morphology of GQDs was investigated by atomic force (AFM) and transmission electron (TEM) microscopies. The size of nanoparticles was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS), while the surface charge was explored by ζ-potential measurement. Structural analysis of samples was conducted by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR). Surface functionalization is also investigated by the back potentiometric titrations. Optical properties were analyzed using UV–VIS and PL spectroscopies. GQDs irradiated at 300 kGy are tested as a potential PL probe for the glyph. The PL intensity of GQDs in the presence of increasing glyph concentration was also measured. The linear relationship between the change of GQDs PL intensity and analyte concentration was investigated according to the Stern-Volmer equation. The limit of detection (LOD) is also calculated. The practical application of GQDs as PL probes for glyph was tested in a real sample (celery). For food sample preparation, a QuEChERS extraction kit was used. In a real sample, a recovery test (R %) for two spiked glyph concentrations was performed. The mechanism of glyph detection was elucidated by FTIR spectroscopy and the Density Functional Theory (DFT) simulations. To estimate the biosafety of modified dots, the cytotoxicity of irradiated GQDs was tested by the MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] and the hemolysis assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and gamma-ray solvent-free modification of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Pristine graphene quantum dots were synthesized by the previously reported, simple, eco-friendly method for electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrodes in the dispersion of NaOH in the 96% EtOH as an electrolyte for the reaction [23]. The obtained material was purified by dialysis in the bags with a molecular weight cutoff (MCWO) of 3.5 kDa. Powdered pristine GQDs (p-GQDs) were collected into 2 mL flasks and exposed to three different doses of gamma irradiation (100 kGy, 200 kGy, and 300 kGy). Samples irradiated in an air medium were redispersed in ultrapure water (resistance 15–18 Ω) for further analysis. Modified samples were labeled as GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair −300 kGy.

The morphology of GQDs was analyzed using atomic force microscopy (AFM). The concentration of all GQD samples was 0.25 mg mL-1. Prepared samples were spin-coated on mica (3500 rpm, 1 min) and recorded using Quesant AFM (Agoura Hills, CA, USA) which was operating in the tapping mode, in the air, at room temperature. A standard silicon tip (NanoAndMore Gmbh, Wetzlar, Germany) was used with a constant force of 40 N m-1. Q-WM300 AFM probe rotated, monolithic silicone probe for non-contact high-frequency application was used. AFM images were analyzed using Gwyddion 2.58 software [46].

Water dispersions of GQDsγair –200 kGy and GQDsγair –300 kGy in the concentration of 1 mg mL-1 were deposited by dropping on lacey carbon copper grids (200 mesh) and dried in air. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) examination of the samples was carried out using a JEOL (Tokyo, Japan) JEM2100F using an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. TEM images were analyzed in Image J 1.54 d software [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering and ζ potential measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and ζ-Potential measurement were acquired at an angle of 173° with a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern) equipped with a 633 nm He–Ne laser. Measurements were performed at a fixed position (4.65 mm) with an automatic attenuator and controlled temperature (20 °C). Before measurements, GDQs (1 mg mL-1) were sonicated with a tip sonicator for 30 minutes in an ice bath. For each sample, ten independent measurements were averaged, and the number-weighted size distribution of the particles was retrieved. Solvent-resistant micro cuvettes (ZEN0040, Malvern, Herrenberg, Germany) have been used for experiments with a sample volume of 40 μL. DLS measures the intensity based on the autocorrelation function:
(1) G2(τ) = 〈I(t+τ)I(t)〉/〈I〉

where τ is the lag time and the brackets represent the ensemble average. The G2 (τ) can be related to the field autocorrelation function g1(t) through the Siegert relation
(2) G2(τ)=1+ β|g1(t)|2

where β is an instrumental constant equal to 1 in our setup. ζ-potential of GDQs was calculated from the electrophoretic mobility using the Henry correction to Smoluchowski's equation, implemented in the Z-sizer data-analysis software. For this purpose, 5 independent measurements at pH=7 were averaged.

XPS spectra of GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy were acquired using a PHI5000 VersaProbe II scanning microprobe (ULVAC-PHI, Chigasaki, Japan), operating with a monochromatic Al Kα source (1486.6 eV). Samples were analyzed with a 100 μm ⌀ beam (25 W) and for each sample both survey scans (Pass Energy, PE = 117.000 eV, energy resolution 1.00 eV, 1100–0 eV) and high resolution for O 1 s and C 1 s regions (PE = 23.500 eV, energy resolution 0.05 eV). The collected electrons were analyzed in FAT mode. The samples were prepared by drop-casting deposition over aluminum foil; during the data acquisition, the sample surface was kept at an angle of 45° with respect to the analyzer; charge neutralization was used during all the experiments by means of both electrons and positive ions (Ar⁺).

For FTIR analysis, powdered samples GQDsγair-100, GQDsγair-200, and GQDsγair-300 kGy, p-GQDs, and GQDsγair-300 kGy/glyph were dispersed in KBr and compressed into pellets. FTIR spectra were recorded using a FTIR Spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet iS20), in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 at 32 scans per spectrum at 4 cm−1 resolution.

For UV-Vis spectroscopy, water dispersion of GQDsγair –100 kGy, GQDsγair –200 kGy, and GQDsγair –300 kGy in the concentration of 0.08 mg mL-1 were prepared. Absorption spectra were measured by a LLG-uniSPEC 2 Spectrophotometer (Lab Logistic group, Mekenheim, Germany) at 25 °C, in air. The wavelength range was 200–800 nm. PL spectra of GQDsγair –100 kGy, GQDsγair–200 kGy, and GQDsγair–300 kGy were recorded on Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 spectrometer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan) at different excitation wavelengths (300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, and 420 nm) in the range of 320–800 nm. GQD samples were prepared in ultrapure water at a concentration of 0.25 mg mL-1. Slits for the emission and the excitation were 8 nm and 2 nm, respectively. The integration time was 0.5 s. Atmospheric pressure and room temperature were used to record all spectra. PL stability of irradiated GQDs was investigated after exposing them to UV light (λ = 365 nm), during the different time intervals (t = 0 min, 15 min, 30 min, 60 min, 90 min, 120 min, 180 min, and 300 min) as well as at various pH values (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 and 13). pH values were adjusted with NaOH and HCl. To obtain values of relative PL quantum yields (PL QY), the integrating areas under the PL emission curves were calculated, and the following Equation was used to estimate PL QY (3):
(3) QYs = QYR(AR/As)(Fs/FR)(ns/nR)2

where QY is PL quantum yield, F is the integrated area under the PL curves of samples or reference, A is the value intensity of the absorbance, n is the refractive index of the solvent, while „R” and “s” are denoted to the selected reference (in this case Rhodamine B, with PL QY of 31%) and samples of gamma irradiated GQDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical analysis of surface functional groups by potentiometric titrations
ce:section.ce:para: Approximately 6 mg of p-GQDs, GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy powder were dissolved in 3.000 mL ofMiliQ water to obtain homogenous dispersions. For determination of acidic moieties concentration, 3.000 mL of GQDs dispersions were mixed with 3.000 mL of the 0.0271 M NaOH or 0.0250 M Na2CO3 and stirred for about 1 h. The excessive base in each sample was quantified by titration with a 0.1031 M HCl standard solution. The same procedure was used for control titrations, except that 3.000 mL of MiliQ water was mixed with acid standard solutions instead of GQDs dispersions. In all titrations, pH values were measured using the Crison pH-Burette 24 2 S equipped with a Hach 52 09 micro combination electrode. Hach standard buffer solutions (pH 4.01, 7.00, and 9.21) were used for electrode calibration. Titrations were performed in argon flow at 25.0±0.1℃ with 10 μL increments and 7 s equilibration time. The concentration of GQDs in all samples was 1 mg mL-1. The total quantity of acidic groups was determined as the difference between the total NaOH and the HCl used up to the first equivalence point. The quantity of carboxylic groups was calculated as the difference between the total Na2CO3 and HCl used up to the first equivalence point. The difference between the total quantity of acidic groups and the quantity of carboxylic groups represents the quantity of phenolic groups. The content of total basic groups was calculated as the difference between total HCl and the NaOH used up to the first equivalence point (see Table S1).

For glyphosate detection, a series of samples in the concentration range of 0–100 μmol L-1 was prepared in MiliQ water. The concentration of GQDsγair-300 kGy in each sample was 0.03 mg mL-1. Photoluminescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of different glyph concentrations was measured on Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 spectrometer at room temperature at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Excitation and emission slits were 8 and 2 nm, respectively. Integration time was 0.5 s.

Aqueous solutions containing individual analytes such as glyph, carbofuran (CF), amitrole (AMT), Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Zn2+ were mixed with water dispersion of GQDsγair-300 kGy (0.03 mg mL-1). The final concentration of each analyte in the sample was 100 μmol L-1. The selectivity of glyph detection was investigated in the presence of several small organic molecules: glucose, citric acid, and L-ascorbic acid (Vit. C). To water dispersion of GQDsγair-300 kGy (0.03 mg mL-1) 100 μmol L-1 of gyph was added, as well as the same concentration of selected molecules. PL spectra were recorded at the previously described conditions.

The practical application of GQDsγair-300 kGy as a PL probe for glyph detection was tested in food samples. Celery was purchased at the local market. About 25 g of celery root was chopped and blended until a homogeneous mass was obtained. Thus, the prepared samples were extracted using QuEChERS (Quick, Easy, Cheap, Effective, Rugged, & Safe) extraction kit (QuEChERS Extract Tubes, EN Method, Agilent Technologies) [48]. It allows the remove of interacting substances such as chlorophyll and carotenoids. For extraction, 10 g of homogenized celery sample was transferred in the 50 mL PTFE centrifuge tube, after which 10 mL of acetonitrile was added. Then, the content of the 6.5 g extraction kit (4 g MgSO4, 1 g Na3C6H5O7, 0.5 g Na2C6H6O7·1.5 H2O and 1 g NaCl) was added. The sample was shaken vigorously for 1 min. After that, the extract was centrifuged for 5 min at 4000 rpm. The supernatant was collected and its pH was adjusted to 7 for future testing. Known concentrations of the glyph (25 and 50 μmol L-1) were prepared by spiking certain amounts of glyph in two aliquots of celery supernatant. As prepared solutions were mixed with GQDsγair-300 kGy dispersion. Then, PL intensities of GQDs were measured at previously described conditions, and recovery (R %) of each tested glyph concentration was calculated.

The Gaussian 09 software was used for all DFT computations [49]. The [C59O27H26]⁻ cluster was utilized as a model system for the DFT calculations to study the adsorption properties of GQD and the electronic structure of different glyph/GQD adducts. This cluster contains 19 fused hexagonal C6-rings, fourteen hydroxy, three epoxy, four carboxyl, and one -COO⁻ group. The ground-state geometry optimizations of pristine GQD and glyph/GQD complexes were performed in the aqueous phase using the ωB97xD hybrid functional [50] in combination with the SMD solvent model [51]. The Pople's valence double-ζ polarized 6–31+G(d,p) basis set was employed for all atoms [52]. Harmonic frequency analyses were carried out at the same level of theory to prove that these structures present local minima on the potential energy surfaces. The counterpoise approach was used to determine the intermolecular interaction energies (ΔE CP) with corrections for basis set superposition errors (BSSE) [53], following the equations:
(4) ΔE = E Complex – (E GQD + E
Glyph )
(5) ΔE CP = ΔE + E BSSE

The negative values of ΔE & ΔE CP indicate that the adsorption process is energetically favorable. GaussView software [54] was used to obtain the optimized geometry of the clusters, ESP maps, and NBO color schemes.

MRC-5 cells (human fetal lung fibroblast cells) were grown in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) High Glucose supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotic/antimycotic solution (all purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) in a humidified atmosphere at 37°C and 5% CO2 in cell incubator.

MRC-5 cells were seeded in 96 well plates at a concentration 5 000 cells per well and left overnight to attach to the plate surface. The next day cells were treated with increasing concentrations of GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, GQDsγair-300 kGy and p-GQDs (1, 10, 25, 50, 75 and 100 µg mL-1). After 24 h of treatment cytotoxicity of tested GQDs was assessed using (3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) (MTT) assay [55,56]. Briefly, the medium was discarded and cells were washed three times with phosphate buffer saline (PBS). Next, cells were incubated with MTT reagent (Merck KGaA, Germany) (5 mg mL-1 in cell culture medium) for 2 hours. Then, formazan crystals were dissolved in DMSO and the absorbance was recorded at 550 nm using a microplate reader Infinite 200 PRO (Tecan, Switzerland).

Human blood samples were collected according to the requirements of the Ethics Committee of the Institute of Molecular Genetics and Genetic Engineering, University of Belgrade (Ethical permission No: O-EO-044/2023). Blood (2.5 mL) was collected in sodium citrate 3.2% tubes to obtain fresh anticoagulant whole blood samples. The samples were centrifuged at 1500xg for 15 min to pallet RBCs (red blood cells). Packed RBCs were washed five times with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and diluted to 25 mL with fresh PBS. In the hemolysis assay, 250 μL of diluted RBCs was mixed with 750 μL of GQDs suspension in PBS to final concentrations of 1, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 µg mL-1. RBSs mixed with distilled water and PBS were used as positive and negative controls, respectively. After incubation at 37 °C for 3 h, samples were centrifuged at 10,000xg for 5 min and aliquots of supernatants were transferred to 96 well plates and used to measure the absorbance values of hemoglobin. Absorbance measurements were performed at 570 nm using a microplate reader Infinite 200 PRO (Tecan, Switzerland). The hemolysis was expressed by the hemolysis ratio calculated using the Eq. 6:
(6) Hemolysis ratio (%) = (ODsample − ODnegative control)/(ODpositive control − ODnegative control)x100%

In Fig. 1. AFM images of GQDsγair-100 kGy (a), GQDsγair-200 kGy (b), and GQDsγair-300 kGy (c) with histograms of height and diameter distributions are presented. From the images, can be observed that GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy are well-dispersed, homogeneously distributed, disc-shaped, and nanometer-sized particles. Their heights vary from 1 nm to 5 nm for dots irradiated at 100 and 300 kGy, while in the sample irradiated at 200 kGy, the highest number of 0.5–1 nm high particles were found. As for diameter, AFM analysis has shown a wide distribution of dots in the range of 30–60 nm, in all samples, which is common for GQDs synthesized by top-down methods [43,57,58].

Average heights and diameters for GQDγair samples are listed in Table 1. Compared to p-GQDs where the average diameter was measured to be 24.0 nm and the height of 1.11 nm [18] the gamma irradiation in selected condition lead to formation of particles with a larger diameter, while the high was similar at doses of 100 and 200 kGy, while it was doubled at 300 kGy. These results suggest that the used condition leads to a connection between neighboring dots, while the number of graphene layers was similar at both 100 and 200 kGy.

Particle size also has been investigated by the TEM analysis. HR-TEM images of the GQDsγair −200 kGy, and GQDsγair −300 kGy are presented in Figs. 2a and 2b, respectively. TEM images show also well-dispersed, homogenously distributed, nano-scaled GQDs. By measuring individual particles, it can be seen that GQDsγair-200 kGy are larger than GQDsγair-300 kGy. Their average diameters obtained by TEM analysis are 47.86 nm, and 9.76 nm, respectively. In the case of p-GQDs, TEM measurement showed that the diameter varied between 32.0 and 20.4 nm [18].

The hydrodynamic diameter of GQDsγair was examined using DLS. The results of the measurements are presented in Fig. 3. From the diameter distribution profiles, it can be seen that all GQDs have a similar monomodal shape of distributions. Average values of hydrodynamic volumes are 59 ± 2.39, 79 ± 0.73, and 97 ± 1.43 nm, for the GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy. Average values were compared on 10 independent replicates of the experiment. In the case of p-GQDs, the average diameter was 13.85 nm, while the zata potential was measured to be -34.6 eV [23]. The hydrodynamic radius of gamma-irradiated GQDs increased as the irradiation dose was increased.

AFM, TEM, and DLS results indicate that different doses of gamma rays affect the GQD morphology. Increasing irradiation dose led to a non-linear increase in particle height and diameter. The lowest diameter was found in GQDs γair-100 kGy (39.17 nm), while for GQDsγair-200 kGy sample is the largest (51.15 nm). This is a consequence of GQDs graphene cores fusion, probably due to the lowest edge functionalization in a sample irradiated at 200 kGy [18]. The similar thickness of GQDs γair-100 and −200 kGy (1.21 and 1.02 nm, respectively) can be explained by low sp2 content (Table 3.), whereby stacking of graphene layers was absent. On the contrary, the sample irradiated at 300 kGy is sp2 reach (Table 3.), which allows π-π interaction between their graphene cores and height increase.

ζ-potential measurement of the GQDsγair ( Table 2.) showed that particles are negatively charged (-12.54 mV, -9.92 mV, -13.78 mV) for GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy and GQDsγair-300 kGy, respectively with negligible standard deviation values. Electron-rich surface in all samples steam from carboxyl groups attached to GQDs. As carboxylic moieties are negatively charged at pH 7, electrostatic repulsions are pronounced causing good GQDs colloid stability with no aggregations. The surface charge of all gamma irradiated samples is larger compared to p-GQDs (-34.6 eV) [23], indicating the lower number of negatively charged functional groups in the samples of GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy and GQDsγair-300 kGy.

The structure of GQDsγair was investigated by XPS and FTIR spectroscopies. The results of XPS measurements are presented in Fig. 4. and 5. It is evident, from the surveys comparison (Fig. 4.) that the prepared samples contain N, Na, and Cl, due to sample preparation. This does not affect significantly the qualitative XPS determinations (N and Cl do not interfere with the investigated areas, and Na KLL band is clearly separable from O 1 s region analysis).

For all investigated spectra, it is possible to observe (C 1 s region, Fig. 5.) that four main contributions can be identified, conserved in all samples: the graphene scaffold (sp2 carbon, 284.40 eV), the C−C/C−H carbon (i.e. carbon not bound to heteroatoms, 284.80 eV; reference for binding energy values), the C−O carbon (such an attribution would encompass both epoxy and hydroxy groups, ≅ 286.3 eV) and the carboxyl group (≅ 289.4 eV). These findings are confirmed by the O 1 s region analysis, showing the presence of both single-bound and double-bound oxygen (C−O, ≅ 533,0 eV, and CO, ≅531.3 eV).

The assignations and relative abundances are reported in Table 3. It is possible to observe from the O/C relative abundance ratio, that there is no significant difference in oxygen intake due to irradiation, while it is evident how the γ-rays interaction affects the overall species distribution (different oxidation states, scaffold modifications). In a previous work was reported that the observed content of graphene-like carbon in p-GQDs was 75% [59]. In our set of samples, it is possible to observe that with the increase of irradiation, we observe a sharp decrease in graphene-like carbon content (from 75% to 11.18% and 14.52%, at doses of 100 kGy and 200 kGy, respectively) and then, at higher doses (300 kGy) a sudden increase (45.74%). This is probably related to defect formation at lower doses, and to reparation of the graphene lattice at higher doses of γ-rays [60]. Such a consideration could be confirmed by the decrease of oxygen-bound carbon in the GQDs at 200 kGy. This phenomenon has already been described for gamma irradiation of graphene oxide (GO) at different doses from 150 to 3000 kGy by Wang et al. [60]. It was observed that at the lower irradiation doses gamma rays induce damage of C atoms and lead to vacancies and defect formation. At the higher doses, the ID/IG ratio obtained by Raman spectroscopy measurement was lowered to 0.89, but it is still higher than at pristine graphene. These results were explained by the diffusion of C atoms and local annealing. It was noticed that the main mechanism of the graphene structural damage by gamma rays is the Compton effect. However, when gamma photons interact with electrons of C atoms, part of the energy is transferred to the electron and it becomes ejected. This electron collides with other C atoms and also transfers the energy. Transferred energy is larger than displacement threshold energy and causes sputtering which is followed by vacancy formation. A collision cascade happens as long as displaced electrons collide with other C atoms. With increase of displaced C atoms, leads to structural disorder and decreases the number of C atoms in the graphene crystal lattice. When the saturation with defects occurred, higher doses of gamma irradiation led to annealing and atom diffusion. The local annealing happens when the energy transfer in the collision is lower than the displacement threshold energy. C atoms vibrate near their equilibrium position. The energy excess is eliminated by the conversion into thermal energy. It causes an increase in local temperature and results in annealing. It results in the moving of ejected C atoms into early-formed vacancies and the reparation of defects in the graphene crystal lattice.

In Fig. 6. FTIR spectra of the p-GQDs (black line) GQDsγair-100 kGy (red line), GQDsγair −200 kGy (blue line), and GQDsγair −300 kGy (green line) are presented. First, a band around 3425 cm-1 is found in the spectra of both, non-irradiated and irradiated GQDs. This band is assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H bonds in hydroxy groups bound on the graphene surface [61]. Bands at 2970, 2927, and 2864 cm-1 detected also in all spectra are typical for graphene materials and steam from stretching vibrations of C-H bonds in -CH and -CH2 domains [57,62]. These bands are the most prominent in the spectra of p-GQDs, while they are less pronounced in the spectra of GQDsγair samples. The change of the band intensity can indicate the disruption of the graphene lattice during irradiation in the air condition at all applied doses. The weak band at 1704 cm-1 located in the p-GQDs spectrum can be attributed to stretching vibrations of CO bonds in the carboxyl functional groups [58]. This band is moved to 1646 cm-1 and became more prominent on spectra of irradiated samples. The shift and enhancement of band intensity can point out the higher amount of carboxyl moieties in the GQDs exposed to γ rays. High-intensity band in the p-GQDs spectrum at 1574 cm-1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of CC bonds in sp2 domains [63]. In the irradiated samples, this band changes position at 1569 in the spectra GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy samples, and at 1564 cm-1 in the GQDsγair-300 kGy sample. A decrease in band intensity is also observed. These differences are the consequence of defects created in the graphene core of γ-ray exposed quantum dots. Bands at 1400, 1332, and 1095 cm-1 are present in all spectra and originated from vibrations C–OH, and symmetric stretching of C–O bonds in epoxy groups in their structure [63].

XPS and FTIR results showed that γ irradiation in the air medium is responsible for the disruption or the restoration of the graphene core of GQDs, depending on the supplied dose. Additionally, with an increase in irradiation dose, the total amount of oxygen in the sample is enhanced too, most probably because the reactive oxygen species formed by radiolysis of O2 molecules in an air medium led to the incorporation of O-moieties in the GQDs structure. If the dose of gamma irradiation is further increased, two scenarios are possible: (1) structural changes would follow the trend in the increase of sp2 domains until the maximum was reached or (2) vacancy formation would start, as was observed previously [60]. For further sensing investigation, we chose GQDsγair-300 kGy due to the highest oxygen content, but also because of the least damaged graphene core during gamma irradiation.

Optical properties of GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy were examined by UV-Vis and PL spectroscopy. In Fig. 7, absorption spectra of the GQDsγair-100 kGy (black curve), GQDsγair-200 kGy (red curve), and GQDsγair-300 kGy (blue curve) are presented. It was previously reported that in the p-GQDs spectrum, an absorption band at 240, and a weak shoulder band at 270 nm were observed [27]. These bands were attributed to π→π*, and n→π* electronic transition in π-conjugated domains, and CO bonds in carboxyl functional groups, respectively. By comparing these results with the spectrum of GQDsγair, some changes can be noticed. First of all, the absorption band at 240 nm in p-GQDs is blue-shifted at 230 nm, 211 nm, and 217 nm on GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy spectra, respectively. The hypsochromic shift of this absorption band may be assigned to the disruption of π-conjugated domains during γ irradiation in the air medium [64]. The weak band at 270 nm did not change position, but it became more pronounced on GQDsγair spectra. This feature implies an increase in carboxyl group content in these samples [65].

In Fig. 8, PL spectra of GQDsγair-100 kGy (a), GQDsγair-200 kGy (b), and GQDsγair-300 kGy (c) recorded at the different excitation wavelengths (λexc 300–420 nm) are presented. With λexc increasing, emission maximums were red-shifted. These shifts were 441 Ex 300- 511 Ex 420 nm, 437 Ex 300-510 Ex 420 nm, 440 Ex 300-510 Ex 420 nm, for GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy, respectively ( Table 4). Such behavior of GQDs is known as excitation-depended PL, and it is usually present in literature [66,67]. It indicates that gamma-irradiated GQDs emit from blue to green light when they are differently excited. For p-GQDs, investigated in previous work, the change of peak position was detected from 430 to 492 nm when they are excited at wavelengths 300–400 nm, and emission from violet-blue to turquoise spectral part [27]. The emission of modified GQDs was shifted to the higher wavelengths when compared with p-GQDs. The changes in optical features of modified dots may have originated from functionalization and higher content of oxygen-contained groups, as a consequence of gamma irradiation in the air medium. It is already known that the PL of GQDs is controlled by both the graphene core (intrinsic emission) and the surface functionalization (extrinsic emission) [68]. Considering that in our case PL peak position between different gamma-irradiated GQDs does not significantly change at the same excitation wavelength, it can be supposed that graphene domains are the dominant PL controlling factor in these samples, instead of differences in surface oxidation level.

Apart from that, the intensities of emission maximums were also changed (Table 4). While for p-GQDs it was found at λexc of 340 nm, same for GQDsγair-100 kGy (a), GQDsγair-200 kGy(b), and GQDsγair-300 kGy was detected at 380 nm.

The values of PL QY were 5.96, 6.92 and 6.94% for GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy, respectively. Compared to PL QY of p-GQDs (2.07%) [23], gamma irradiation increased around three times PL QY at all applied doses.

PL intensity maximums of GQDsγair-100 kGy (a), GQDsγair-200 kGy (b), and GQDsγair-300 kGy samples (c) recorded in the pH range from 1 to 13, are shown in Fig. 9. It can be noticed that the PL of irradiated GQDs is relatively stable in neutral and alkaline media (pH 7–13), while stability is drastically decreasing in acidic conditions, due to particle aggregation [19].

Fig. 10. represents a PL maximum intensities obtained by investigation of GQDsγair-100 kGy (a), GQDsγair-200 kGy (b), and GQDsγair-300 kGy PL stability after their UV light irradiation (λ = 365 nm) at a different time interval, from 0 to 300 minutes. During the tested intervals, a significant change in PL intensity was not observed in any spectrum. Considering that pH and photostability are important parameters for practical applications, it can be assumed that the fluorescence of gamma-irradiated GQDs is enough stable for potential use in optical detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical analysis of surface oxygen functional groups by potentiometric titrations
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR and XPS analysis of GQDsγair samples show the presence of various oxygen functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, phenolic, and epoxy in their structure. Another method for the identification and quantification of sample moiety types in samples is acid-base titration [69]. This method is based on the neutralization of acidic/basic functional groups with an excess of an appropriate base/acid, and subsequent back-titration of the unreacted base/acid. For determinations of acidic groups, different bases are used. NaOH, as a strong base, can neutralize both phenolic and carboxylic groups, while Na2CO3 can neutralize only carboxylic groups. Titration curves for p-GQDs samples equilibrated with NaOH (a) or Na2CO3 (b) and back-titrated with standard HCl solution are shown in Fig. 11 along with blank NaOH/Na2CO3 + HCl “blank“ titrations. These results indicate the presence of acidic and basic functional groups in GQD samples. Similar titration curves are obtained for irradiated samples (Figure S1).

Results for p-GQDs, GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy, given as total quantity of functional groups on dots per 6.000 mL of 1.00 mg mL-1 dots dispersion, are shown in Table 5. Obtained values indicate that in p-GQDs, and GQDsγair-300 kGy, carboxylic and basic groups are found, while in the other two samples, phenolic groups were detected as well. The content of carboxylic groups is increased in all irradiated dots. These results can be explained by oxidation of the GQDs edges during irradiation in an air medium, whereby phenolic groups were first attacked. With an increase in the irradiation dose and generation of oxygen-contained radicals by radiolysis of O2 molecules from the air, these radicals can be transformed into various oxygen-containing groups, among them carboxylic [70]. Furthermore, the total content of basic groups decreased as the irradiation dose was increased. The results of back-potentiometric titrations are in agreement with those obtained by XPS and FTIR spectroscopies, as well as ξ-potential values. The selected methods for structural analyses have proven the trend of increasing oxygen moieties by the proposed modification approach.

PL spectra of GQDsγair-in the presence of herbicide glyphosate are shown in Fig. 12 (a). The PL intensities of GQDs were significantly decreased with the increase in glyph concentration (Fig. 12a). It is observed that glyph quenched a PL intensity of GQDs and it was declared as a turn-off detection mechanism [17,23]. The values of PL intensities at selected glyphosate concentrations are presented in Supporting Information, Tables 2S and 3S. Quenching efficiency was investigated by linear fitting of PL intensity versus the glyph concentration data (Fig. 12b). Results of linear fitting are described by the Stern-Volmer equation A0/A= 1+KSV [Q] [16], where A0 and A represent integrated areas under the GQDs’ PL emission curves in the absence and the presence of quencher, respectively, KSV is the quenching constant which represents a slope of the fitting curve, and Q is the concentration of quencher. Stern-Volmer equation, in this case, is A0/A = 1.0577+ 0.00613 [glyph].

Linearity was detected between 10 and 40 μmol L-1 of glyph (Fig. 12b, inset). The obtained coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.99 value, indicates a good linear relationship between GQDsγair-300 kGy and glyph. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by relation LOD =3σ/KSV, where 3 is the signal-to-noise ratio, while σ is the standard deviation of the intercept. The obtained values of LOD and σ are 3.02 μmol L-1 and 0.006, respectively.

The range of linearity, R2, SD, KSV, and LOD obtained in this work are compared with values reported in the literature. Results are summarized in Table 6. From the presented data can be seen that the LOD obtained in our work is within the range of the reported values.

Maxima of GQDsγair-300 kGy PL intensity in the absence (blank), and the presence of the pesticides (glyph, CF, AMT) and ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Zn2+) which usually are presented in the agricultural field are shown in Fig. 13 (a). Only the glyph led to a decrease of GQDs PL intensity, which makes them potential PL probes selective for the glyph. In Fig. 13 (b), the PL intensities of the mixture GQDsγair-300 kGy with gyph, in the present of small organic molecules, glucose, citric acid, and Vit. C is presented. While both glucose and citric acid have very minor effects on the PL intensity of the mixture of GQDsγair-300 kGy with the glyph (with less than 2.4%), Vit. C caused a PL intensity of 3.9%. These results indicate that GQDsγair-300 kGy are able to detect gyph in the present of studied molecules.

For evaluation of the proposed method's applicability, a detection of glyph using GQDsγair-300 kGy as the PL probe was tested in a food sample. The supernatant obtained by purification of the celery sample using a QuEChERS extraction kit ( Fig. 14.) was used for recovery analysis.

Glyphosate in concentrations of 25 and 50 μmol L-1 was spiked in celery samples (Supporting Information, Table 4S and Figure S2), and mixed with GQDsγair-300 kGy, in which PL intensities were measured. Then, the recoveries of spiked samples were found. Measurements were acquired in triplicates. As results in Table 7 indicate, average values of recovery (R av. (%)) were 70 and 107%, for 25 and 50 μmol L-1 of glyph, respectively. These values are within the acceptable range of recovery (70 – 120%) [75]. Relative standard deviations (RSDs) are less than 3% in both cases, which indicates well reproducibility of the studied optical method [30].

FTIR spectra of GQDsγair-300 kGy in the absence (black line) and the presence (red line) of the glyph are shown in Fig. 15. The bands found on the GQDsγair-300 kGy spectrum have already been shown in Fig. 5. After the addition of glyph to the system, the spectrum of GQDsγair-300 kGy has changed. It can be seen that bands at 3439, 2970, 2927, 2864 cm-1, and 1400 cm-1 in the GQDsγair-300 kGy/glyph spectrum did not change the positions or intensity, but new bands at 916 and 776 cm-1 are detected. These bands indicate the loading of herbicide onto GQD surface, and steam from asymmetrical and symmetrical vibrations of P-O bonds in phosphate functional groups in the glyph, respectively [76]. Bands at 1646 and 1095 cm-1 that originated from carboxyl and epoxy functional groups, became pronounced and shifted to 1632 and 1080 cm-1 in GQDsγair-300 kGy/glyph spectrum, respectively. Based on previously reported results, obtained by analysis of glyph absorption on the graphene surface, these changes suggested hydrogen bonding [68]. Additionally, the higher intensity of the band at 1332 cm-1 from C-OH bonds compared to the same band in the GQDsγair-300 kGy spectrum, is also observed. These results indicate that the carboxyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl groups are included in the hydrogen bond formed between GQDsγair-300 kGy and glyph. At pH 7, the glyph behaves as a weak acid, so it can form hydrogen bonds with oxygen-containing groups of GQDs [77].

Calculations of binding energies are crucial for assessing the stability and strength of the intermolecular noncovalent interactions in glyph/GQD complexes. The optimized structures of five glyph/GQD adducts are shown in Fig. 16, while the calculated binding energies (ΔE & ΔE CP) are summarized in Table 8. The top views of these five adducts are shown in ESI (Figure S3). According to zeta potential analysis, GQDs are negatively charged (-13.8 mV) at pH 7 due to the edge phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, whereas glyphosate is in the di-anionic form at neutral pH=7. Thus, it is considered the GQD cluster and glyphosate molecule are monoanionic and di-anionic species, respectively. To estimate the adsorption energies of different glyph conformers at the GQD surface, we selected two binding sites of the GQD: edge (E) and basal plane (S). The edges of the GQD are capped with carboxyl and phenolic (-OH) groups, while the surface is covered with hydroxyl and epoxy groups. The glyph molecules interact with the GQD via hydrogen bonds, serving as H-donors and H-acceptors. The computed H-bond distances, which vary from 1.41 to 2.40 Å (Fig. 16), could be categorized as strong hydrogen bonding according to Jeffery et al. (< 2.5 Å; strong, mainly covalent) [78]. The highest interaction energy was calculated for complex S1 (ΔE CP = -18.33 kcal mol-1), while the weakest binding was found in complex E3 (ΔE CP = -12.61 kcal mol-1). This is reasonable because surface complexes can establish more hydrogen bonds than edge conformations. However, adducts E1 and E2 are not pure edge conformers; there are also additional H-bonds with surface hydroxyl groups, resulting in higher interaction energies than E3 (Table 6).

Since both species (GQD and glyph) are negatively charged, this is an example of when the hydrogen bondings win over the Coulomb repulsion. The "shielding effect" of the solvent, which reduces the effective charges of the interacting species, also supports this.

The thermodynamic data of the interactions between glyph and GQD at 298.15 K and 1 atm obtained by DFT calculations at the ωB97xD/6–31+G(d,p)/SMD(water) level are also presented in Table 8. It can be noticed that the relative order of the ΔG and ΔH of the glyph/GQD complexes is consistent with those of the ΔE CP. The results indicate that the binding process of glyph and GQD in aqueous solution is exergonic (spontaneous), with ΔG values of -25.31, -21.24, and -2.34 kJ mol-1 for E1, E2, and E3, respectively. For the surface complexes ΔG values are -29.89, and -23.94 kJ mol-1, for S1 and S2, respectively. The ΔG values also point to a tendency for glyph molecules to bind to GQD surfaces rather than their edges. Following the ΔH values, all the glyph /GQD adduct formations are exothermic. The thermodynamic information shows that the binding of GQD by glyph in water is enthalpy-driven, which is also experimentally proved.

As previously mentioned, ζ potential analysis indicates that GQDs have a negative charge of -13.8 mV. This is due to the presence of phenolic (-OH) and carboxyl groups at the edges, confirmed by FTIR and UV–vis spectroscopic results [79]. On the other hand, the glyphosate molecules are in di-anionic form at an operational pH value (∼ 7.0). Therefore, we introduced one negative charge into the GQD cluster through the carboxyl group (-COO⁻), whereas the glyph molecule is determined in di-anionic form. The Mulliken (Q Mulliken ) and natural bond orbital (Q NBO ) analyses have been carried out to evaluate the magnitude and direction of charge transfer inside glyph/GQD complexes, and the results are illustrated in Fig. 17b and summarized in Table 8. All these results (Q Mulliken & Q NBO ) point to an electron-accepting property of GQD in glyph/GQD complexes by indicating a charge transfer from glyph to GQDs. The charge transfer is negligible within the E3 (edge) complex, considering both Q Mulliken & Q NBO results. For other complexes, according to Q NBO , the GQD cluster accepts electron density of 0.13 – 0.24 e⁻ from the glyph, while based on Q Mulliken , it adopts from 0.16 to 0.28 e⁻ ( Table 9). The dipole moments (Debye) are also depicted in Fig. 17. It can be seen that the glyph/GQD complexes possess higher dipole moments than separated constituents, owing to the higher charge separation within the complexes. Dipole moments reflect charge separation rather than charge transfers, consequently, charge transfers do not follow the magnitude of dipole moments.

Fig. 17a shows electrostatic potential maps (MEP) of the two most stable glyph/GQD complexes (E1 & S1) and their constituents. The MEP values are depicted as follows: red and blue represent the electron-rich (negative) and electron-deficient (positive) parts, respectively, while green designates areas with zero potential. The negative (red) regions of the glyph and GQD are around the -PO3 2- and -COO⁻ groups, respectively. The MEP also reveals that the electropositive (blue) regions are at the edges of the bare GQD cluster. On the other side, within the glyph/GQD complexes (E1 & S1) edges of the clusters are yellowish, and colors are generally shifted toward the red region, indicating that GQDs accept the electron densities from glyph molecules. Most of the negative charge remains in the phosphorus group of the glyph.

Both, FTIR spectroscopy and DFT analysis have given insight into the interactions in the GQDsγair-300 kGy/glyph system which led to a decrease in GQDs PL intensity. FTIR analysis proved a formation of hydrogen bonds between GQDs' hydroxyl, epoxy, and carboxyl groups, and glyph molecule. The DFT calculations indicate that hydrogen bonding formations (according to H-bonding distances and binding energies) overcome the electrostatic repulsion in the system. Yuan and co-workers developed a potential PL sensor for glyph, based on carbon dots (CDs) [77]. They observed that in the presence of increasing glyph concentration, the PL of the CDs decreased. PL quenching is attributed to hydrogen bond formation between oxygen groups at the CD surface and the phosphonic groups of the glyph at pH 7. To resolve the quenching mechanism in detail, they measured the absorbance of the glyph and the PL of CDs. As the absorbance spectra of the glyph overlapped with CDs PL spectra, it was concluded that the PL quenching is a consequence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from CDs to glyph. In our case, the absorbance spectrum of glyph did not show an overlap with the emission spectrum of GQDsγair-300 kGy (see Figure S4), so FRET could not be considered as a potential mechanism of PL quenching. In another publication, Nair et al. investigated the mechanism of S-GQDs PL quenching in the presence of insecticide carbofuran (CF) [80]. They observed that the carboxyl and sulphonic groups of S-GQDs and the CF carbamate group formed a complex through hydrogen bonding. It was found that upon excitation, electrons in the complex passed from lower energy ground to higher energy excited state, with photon absorption. Returning to the ground state, energy in the form of heat is released into the surrounding medium. The results obtained in our work support that type of mechanism.

To investigate the potential cytotoxic effect of p-GQDs, GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy and GQDsγair-300 kGy MRC-5 cells were treated with increasing concentrations of tested GQDs (1 µg mL-1, 10 µg mL-1, 25 µg mL-1, 50 µg mL-1, 75 µg mL-1, and 100 µg mL-1) for 24 h and cell viability was measured using MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 18, all classes of GQDs, at all tested concentrations didn’t show a cytotoxic effect in the MTT assays in comparison to the vehicle control (cell viability >70%) (Fig. 18).

To evaluate the biocompatibility of GQDsγair-100 kGy, GQDsγair-200 kGy, and GQDsγair-300 kGy, and p-GQDs hemolysis assay was performed ( Fig. 19). Human RBCs were incubated with different concentrations of tested GQDs (1 µg mL-1, 10 µg mL-1, 25 µg mL-1, 50 µg mL-1, 75 µg mL-1, and 100 µg mL-1), and hemolysis rate was calculated. As presented, all samples showed a hemolysis rate of less than 5% which classified them as non-hemolytic (Fig. 19 a, b, c, and d). In line with these results visual inspection of the upper liquid layer, after centrifugation of RBC samples treated with tested GQDs, showed color ranged from clear to pale yellow without hemoglobin outflow (Fig. 19).

An original, ultra-fast, solvent-free, and eco-friendly method for GQD structural transformation with gamma rays in an air medium is presented. Structural analysis and potentiometric titration results showed the highest content of carboxylic moieties in GQDs irradiated at a dose of 300 kGy. The blue-green luminescent, oxygen-rich GQDsγair-300 kGy with stable PL were successfully used as a direct turn-off sensor for non-selective herbicide glyph, with a low LOD value of 3.02 μmol L-1. Among several tested pollutants, GQDs showed selectivity toward glyphosate. Glyphosate has been detected in the celery root, prepared using QuEchERS extraction kits to remove interfering compounds. FTIR spectroscopy proved a hydrogen bond formation between GQDs and glyphosate and confirmed by DFT calculations. The mechanism of GQDs PL quenching is based on releasing the energy in the form of heat, rather than PL, when an electron in the GQDs/glyph complex was photoexcited. MTT and hemolysis assays showed that GQDs do not have a negative biological impact on tested cell lines. In conclusion, these results demonstrate the potential of our dispersible and non-toxic γ-irradiated GQDs for fast, simple, efficient, and environmentally friendly glyphosate detection.
The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.
